== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).

== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家}}

[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[Category:Qq]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行}} (1080–1162) was the first head. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).

[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[Category:Qq]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).

=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|qq=}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|qq=}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|qq=}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|qq=}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|qq=}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵|qq=}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵|qq=}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美|qq=}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則|qq=}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|qq=}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|qq=}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|qq=}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|qq=}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家|qq=}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|qq=}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|qq=}} {{Tōjō Families list|qq=}}

revision.comment: 173 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:清華家 up to rev 103419371
revision.text:
'''清華家'''（せいがけ）は、[[公家]]の[[家格]]のひとつ。最上位の[[五摂家]]に次ぎ、[[大臣家]]の上の序列に位置する。大臣・大将を兼ねて[[太政大臣]]になることのできる主に7家（[[三条家|三条]]・[[西園寺家|西園寺]]・[[徳大寺家|徳大寺]]・[[久我家|久我]]・[[花山院家|花山院]]・[[大炊御門家|大炊御門]]・[[菊亭家|菊亭]]）を指す（室町時代には10家あった。また江戸期には[[広幡家|広幡]]・[[醍醐家|醍醐]]の両家を加えて9家など、時代によって[[家格]]を有した家の数が異なる）。
== 成立過程 == 英雄家、華族ともいう。摂家と清華家の子弟は、[[公達]]（きんだち）と呼ばれた。
[[後三条天皇]]の御世以降<ref>[[一条天皇]]御世以降に限れば、[[藤原氏|藤原]][[朝臣]]（[[藤原北家]]）以外で大臣・大将となった例は、初期に[[宇多源氏]]（[[源氏|源朝臣]]）の[[左大臣]][[源雅信]]・[[源重信|重信]]があり、末期には後の清華家[[久我家]]に連なる[[村上源氏]]（源朝臣）の[[右大臣]][[源師房]]がある程度である。摂関経験者（及び[[藤原師輔]]）の子弟・養子以外の藤原氏に広げれば、[[近衛大将|左大将]][[藤原済時]]の例があり、娘の[[藤原娍子]]は[[三条天皇]]の皇后に立てられた。</ref>、[[摂家|摂関家]]が[[外戚]]の地位を失い、代わって外戚となった[[家系]]が、のちに清華家と呼ばれることになる家格の原形をつくった。清華家に相当する家格はすでに院政期には成立している。したがって、清華家の家格は大臣・大将に昇進できるということのほかに「娘が皇后になる資格がある」という点もある。
いわゆる「七清華」は、清華家の家格を有する多数の家系（たとえば藤原北家[[閑院流]]の[[山階家]]・[[洞院家]]、[[村上源氏]][[源顕房|顕房流]]の[[土御門家#土御門家（村上源氏）|土御門家]]・[[堀川家]]）が[[中世]]を通じて絶家したり清華の家格を失ったりした結果、最終的に7家しか残らなかったことを意味しており、はじめから家系が固定していたわけではない。
なお、[[豊臣政権]]下においては、[[五大老]]の[[徳川氏|徳川]]・[[毛利氏|毛利]]・[[小早川氏|小早川]]・[[前田氏#加賀前田家|前田]]・[[宇喜多氏|宇喜多]]・[[上杉氏|上杉]]らも清華成を果たしたとされ、清華家と同等の扱い（[[武家]]清華家）を受けた。
== 昇進過程 == === 鎌倉時代から安土桃山時代 === [[近衛大将|左・右近衛大将]]・[[大臣]]を兼任し、[[内大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[左大臣]]を経て、最高は[[太政大臣]]まで昇進できるが、清華家の家格が誕生した[[鎌倉時代]]初期は後世に残った[[清華家#七家|七清華]]と清華の家格を保持していた家々は、当初は参議に任官された後に中納言、大納言、内・右・左大臣・太政大臣まで昇進していた。後に摂家同様に参議を経ずして中納言に任ぜられる慣例が成立したのは室町時代初期頃（[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]）になる。<ref>ただし、当初は[[蔵人頭]]を経ずに参議に任ぜられる慣例であったとする説（旧・七清華の当主は[[室町時代]]初期まで[[参議]][[任官]]例があり、[[元和 (日本)|元和]]5年（[[1619年]]）に[[西園寺実晴]]と[[花山院定好]]が任ぜられたのが、例外であり最後となっている。）[[坂田桂一]] 「中世前期における清華の家格とその昇進」（『文化学年報』61号、[[2012年]]）</ref>
=== 江戸時代 === [[江戸時代]]においては、[[従五位|従五位下]][[侍従]]を振り出しに{{Efn|摂家は[[正五位|正五位下]][[近衛府|近衛権少将]]。}}、[[近衛府|近衛権中将]]、[[中納言|権中納言]]{{Efn|摂家と同様、近衛中将から[[参議]]を経ずして[[中納言]]に[[任官|任ぜられる]]慣例となっている。}}・[[大納言|権大納言]]を経て、[[近衛大将|右近衛大将]]{{Efn|[[後白河天皇|後白河]][[院政]]末期から[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽]]院政期にかけ、摂関家の[[嫡男|嫡子]]が左大将、清華家の大納言筆頭者が右大将に[[任官]]することが例となったとされる。}}を兼ね大臣に至る。
ただし、[[江戸時代]]の太政大臣は[[摂政]]・[[関白]]経験者（[[摂家]]）に限られ、清華家から出ることはなかった{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=17}}。清華家の[[極位極官|極官]]は事実上[[左大臣]]であったが、江戸時代期を通じてわずか10例と少なく、老齢の当主が名誉的に任じられたケースばかりで在任期間も短い{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=18-19}}。[[大炊御門経光]]はわずか1日の在任であり、最長の[[花山院定好]]でも1年8ヶ月である。さらに寛延2年（1749年）の[[醍醐冬熙]]を最後に幕末まで清華家の左大臣は出ることはなかった{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=18-19}}。他の時期でも三公を摂家が占める例が多く、さらに摂家の威信拡大により清華家の大臣は評議にすら参加しないことが先例とされていた時期もあった{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=21-22}}。江戸時代には清華家の極官について、摂関家側は左大臣、清華家側は太政大臣と認識していて、認識の対立があった可能性も指摘されている<ref>[[長坂良宏]]『近世の摂家と朝幕関係』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[2018年]]） pp. 76 - 77・80・107 - 108.</ref>。
=== 明治時代から華族令廃止まで === [[1884年]]（[[明治]]17年）の[[華族令]]によって、清華の家格の家は一律に[[侯爵]]に叙されることになったが、三条家のみ[[三条実美]]の功績により[[公爵]]とされた。その後、西園寺家は[[西園寺公望]]の、徳大寺家は[[徳大寺実則]]の功績により公爵となった。
== 清華家の一覧 == === 七家 === ; [[三条家]]（転法輪家） : [[藤原北家]][[閑院流]]（太政大臣[[藤原公季]]の子孫）。[[大納言|権大納言]][[藤原公実|公実]]の二男、[[太政大臣]][[三条実行]]（[[1080年]] - [[1162年]]）が初代。清華家に次ぐ[[大臣家]]に三条家の一族を配することで権力を保った。 : [[庶家|庶流]]は大臣家の[[正親町三条家|嵯峨家]]や[[三条西家|三条西]]を始め、[[羽林家]]の[[滋野井家]]、[[姉小路家]] 等、23家にのぼる。 : [[家業]]：笛・装束。[[江戸時代]]の[[家禄]]：469石、[[家紋]]：[[唐花紋|片喰に唐花]]。近代の爵位：[[公爵]]。
; [[西園寺家]] : 藤原北家閑院流。同じく公実の三男、[[中納言|権中納言]][[藤原通季|西園寺通季]]（[[1090年]] - [[1128年]]）を祖とする。通季は母藤原光子が正妻だったため嫡子とされたが、早世したために兄弟の中でも官位が最も低かった。4代目の太政大臣[[西園寺公経|公経]]に至って、親幕派として[[承久の乱]]後権勢を誇り、[[摂家|摂関家]]から[[外戚]]の地位と[[関東申次]]の世襲職を奪った。公経は京洛北山に氏寺西園寺を建立して、家名の由来となった。 : 庶流に菊亭家、羽林家の[[清水谷家]]や[[四辻家]]や[[橋本家]]、[[大宮家 (閑院流)|大宮家]]等あり。 : 家業：[[琵琶]]。江戸時代の家禄：597石、家紋：[[巴|尾長左三つ巴]]。近代の爵位：侯爵 → 公爵。
; [[徳大寺家]] : 藤原北家閑院流。公実の四男、[[左大臣]][[徳大寺実能]]（[[1096年]] - [[1157年]]）を始祖とする。実能の嫡孫、左大臣[[徳大寺実定|実定]]は[[藤原俊成]]の妹が生んだ子で[[藤原定家|定家]]の従兄にあたり、自らも優れた歌人である。この家は[[鳥羽天皇|鳥羽]]・[[後白河天皇|後白河]][[院政]]期の[[後宮]]をほぼ独占したが、[[鎌倉時代|鎌倉]]以後やや衰えた。西園寺家と同族意識が強く、たとえば明治時代の[[西園寺公望]]は徳大寺家に生まれ西園寺家に入嗣している。 : 家業：笛、江戸時代の家禄：約410石、家紋：木瓜唐花角浮線綾。近代の爵位：侯爵 → 公爵。
; [[久我家]] : [[村上天皇]]の第8[[皇子]][[具平親王]]の男、[[右大臣]][[源師房]]（[[1008年]] - [[1077年]]）を祖とする[[村上源氏]]の嫡流。師房の姉・[[隆姫女王]]が[[関白]][[藤原頼通]]の正妻であることから頼通の異姓養子となり、頼通の異母妹[[藤原尊子 (藤原道長女)|尊子]]と結婚する。さらに師房と尊子の子・[[源顕房]]は娘の[[藤原賢子|賢子]]を[[藤原師実]]の養女とした上で[[白河天皇]]に入内させ（のち[[中宮]]）、もう一人の娘・[[藤原師子|師子]]は[[藤原忠実]]の妻となるなど、その子孫も摂関家と深い姻戚関係を築いた。 : 師房の5世孫である[[内大臣]][[源通親|通親]]は[[高階栄子|丹後局]]と組んで時の関白[[九条兼実]]を追い落とし、「{{読み仮名|'''源博陸'''|みなもと の はくりく}}」とあだ名されるほどの権勢家であった。久我家嫡流は長く[[源氏長者]]や[[淳和奨学両院別当]]を兼任したが、[[室町時代]]に入ると[[足利義満]]が源氏長者となり、[[足利将軍家]]が源氏長者となる慣例が成立する。ただし[[足利将軍家]]そのものが後継者争いなどによって不安定な状況が続いたため、実際には[[村上源氏]]公家の久我家と[[清和源氏]]武家の足利家が交互に源氏長者に就任する様相を呈し、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に入ると再び久我家が源氏長者を独占して[[久我通堅]]まで続いた。 : 「久我」の名称は、京都西南、[[山城国]][[乙訓郡]]久我（現在の[[京都市]][[伏見区]]久我）の地に別業「久我水閣」があったことが由来。 : 分家の公家諸家として、大臣家の[[中院家]]、および羽林家の[[六条家]]・[[岩倉家]]・[[千種家]]・[[東久世家]]・[[久世家]]・[[梅溪家]]・[[愛宕家]]・[[植松家]]の9家がある。 : 家業：笛、江戸時代の家禄：700石、家紋：五つ竜胆車。近代の爵位：侯爵。
; [[花山院家]] : 藤原北家師実流（花山院流）。摂政太政大臣[[藤原師実|師実]]の次子、右大臣[[花山院家忠]]（[[1062年]] - [[1136年]]）より始まる。家忠が[[花山天皇|花山上皇]]の御在所だった東一条院（[[花山院]]）を伝領したため、この家名がある。3代[[花山院忠雅|忠雅]]は朝政に明るかった上に、[[平清盛]]と親戚関係にあったことから、太政大臣という異例の昇進を遂げた。諸流に羽林家の[[中山家]]や[[野宮家]]、[[今城家]]がある。 : 家業：[[笙]]と[[筆道]]、江戸時代の家禄：約750石、家紋：[[アヤメ|菖蒲菱]]<ref name="ayame">[https://www.mitsuminejinja.or.jp/saijin/ 御祭神・由緒 | 三峯神社]</ref>。近代の爵位：侯爵。
; [[大炊御門家]] : 藤原北家師実流（花山院流）。花山院家と同じく摂政師実の子[[大炊御門経実]]（[[1068年]] - [[1131年]]）が初代。大炊御門北、万里小路東に邸宅があった。経実は権大納言どまりであったが、その子[[大炊御門経宗|経宗]]は[[二条天皇]]の{{読み仮名|[[外舅]]|がいきゅう}}として勢威をふるい、左大臣に昇って清華家の[[家格]]を確保した（経宗は閑院流公実の女公子を母とする）。 : 家業：筆道・[[和歌]]・[[和琴]]・笛・装束、江戸時代の家禄：約400石、家紋：菱に片喰草。近代の爵位：侯爵。
; [[菊亭家]]（今出川家） : 藤原北家閑院流西園寺庶流。[[鎌倉時代|鎌倉期]]の太政大臣[[西園寺実兼]]の子、左大臣[[今出川兼季|兼季]]が分家し、今出川殿を居所としたため、今出川および号として菊亭を名乗る。[[明治維新]]後は苗字の統一を図り、菊亭を名字とした。 : 家業：琵琶。:江戸時代の家禄：約1655石、家紋：三つ楓。近代の爵位：侯爵。
=== 新家 === [[江戸時代]]に七清華から2つの[[新家]]を加えたものである。しかしこの両家より[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の最高職である太政大臣に昇った例が一例もなく、既存の七清華よりも格下とされた。近代の爵位は共に侯爵であった。
; [[広幡家]] : [[源氏#正親町源氏|正親町源氏]][[嫡流]]。[[正親町天皇]]の皇孫[[八条宮智仁親王]]の第3[[王 (皇族)|王子]]、[[広幡忠幸|忠幸王]]（[[1623年]] - [[1669年]]）が[[臣籍降下]]して創立。忠幸は最初、[[尾張藩]]に赴き[[武家]]となったが、のち帰洛して大納言に進んだ。 : 禄高：約500石、家紋：[[菊花紋章|三つ葉菊]]
; [[醍醐家]] : 藤原北家摂関流。江戸時代に五摂家の[[一条家]]から分かれた家。摂政[[一条昭良]]の次男、権大納言[[醍醐冬基]]（[[1648年]] - [[1697年]]）が初代。 : 禄高：約312石、家紋：[[フジ (植物)#人間との関わり|下り藤]]
=== 絶家となった家 === ; [[洞院家]] : 藤原北家閑院流西園寺庶流。第 9代の[[洞院実熙]]の時には既に経済的に困窮。第10代[[洞院公数|公数]]は自ら絶家させる選択する。
; [[土御門家#土御門家（村上源氏）|土御門家]] : 村上源氏久我庶流。[[室町時代]]中期に[[土御門有通|有通]]が早世し絶家した。
; [[堀川家]] : 村上源氏。室町時代に絶家した。
; [[松殿家]] : 元は[[摂家]]として創設された家のひとつ。藤原氏北家嫡流の[[藤原忠通]]次男・[[松殿基房]]が祖で、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に絶家、その後[[明和]]2年（[[1765年]]）に[[九条尚実]]の次男忠孝が九条家の分家として、清華家待遇となる松殿家を創設したが、明和5年（[[1768年]]）に嗣子無く没し、再び絶家した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[清華家]] to [[Seiga families]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''清華家'''（せいがけ）は、[[公家]]の[[家格]]のひとつ。最上位の[[五摂家]]に次ぎ、[[大臣家]]の上の序列に位置する。大臣・大将を兼ねて[[太政大臣]]になることのできる主に7家（[[三条家|三条]]・[[西園寺家|西園寺]]・[[徳大寺家|徳大寺]]・[[久我家|久我]]・[[花山院家|花山院]]・[[大炊御門家|大炊御門]]・[[菊亭家|菊亭]]）を指す（室町時代には10家あった。また江戸期には[[広幡家|広幡]]・[[醍醐家|醍醐]]の両家を加えて9家など、時代によって[[家格]]を有した家の数が異なる）。
== 成立過程 == 英雄家、華族ともいう。摂家と清華家の子弟は、[[公達]]（きんだち）と呼ばれた。
[[後三条天皇]]の御世以降<ref>[[一条天皇]]御世以降に限れば、[[藤原氏|藤原]][[朝臣]]（[[藤原北家]]）以外で大臣・大将となった例は、初期に[[宇多源氏]]（[[源氏|源朝臣]]）の[[左大臣]][[源雅信]]・[[源重信|重信]]があり、末期には後の清華家[[久我家]]に連なる[[村上源氏]]（源朝臣）の[[右大臣]][[源師房]]がある程度である。摂関経験者（及び[[藤原師輔]]）の子弟・養子以外の藤原氏に広げれば、[[近衛大将|左大将]][[藤原済時]]の例があり、娘の[[藤原娍子]]は[[三条天皇]]の皇后に立てられた。</ref>、[[摂家|摂関家]]が[[外戚]]の地位を失い、代わって外戚となった[[家系]]が、のちに清華家と呼ばれることになる家格の原形をつくった。清華家に相当する家格はすでに院政期には成立している。したがって、清華家の家格は大臣・大将に昇進できるということのほかに「娘が皇后になる資格がある」という点もある。
いわゆる「七清華」は、清華家の家格を有する多数の家系（たとえば藤原北家[[閑院流]]の[[山階家]]・[[洞院家]]、[[村上源氏]][[源顕房|顕房流]]の[[土御門家#土御門家（村上源氏）|土御門家]]・[[堀川家]]）が[[中世]]を通じて絶家したり清華の家格を失ったりした結果、最終的に7家しか残らなかったことを意味しており、はじめから家系が固定していたわけではない。
なお、[[豊臣政権]]下においては、[[五大老]]の[[徳川氏|徳川]]・[[毛利氏|毛利]]・[[小早川氏|小早川]]・[[前田氏#加賀前田家|前田]]・[[宇喜多氏|宇喜多]]・[[上杉氏|上杉]]らも清華成を果たしたとされ、清華家と同等の扱い（[[武家]]清華家）を受けた。
== 昇進過程 == === 鎌倉時代から安土桃山時代 === [[近衛大将|左・右近衛大将]]・[[大臣]]を兼任し、[[内大臣]]・[[右大臣]]・[[左大臣]]を経て、最高は[[太政大臣]]まで昇進できるが、清華家の家格が誕生した[[鎌倉時代]]初期は後世に残った[[清華家#七家|七清華]]と清華の家格を保持していた家々は、当初は参議に任官された後に中納言、大納言、内・右・左大臣・太政大臣まで昇進していた。後に摂家同様に参議を経ずして中納言に任ぜられる慣例が成立したのは室町時代初期頃（[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]）になる。<ref>ただし、当初は[[蔵人頭]]を経ずに参議に任ぜられる慣例であったとする説（旧・七清華の当主は[[室町時代]]初期まで[[参議]][[任官]]例があり、[[元和 (日本)|元和]]5年（[[1619年]]）に[[西園寺実晴]]と[[花山院定好]]が任ぜられたのが、例外であり最後となっている。）[[坂田桂一]] 「中世前期における清華の家格とその昇進」（『文化学年報』61号、[[2012年]]）</ref>
=== 江戸時代 === [[江戸時代]]においては、[[従五位|従五位下]][[侍従]]を振り出しに{{Efn|摂家は[[正五位|正五位下]][[近衛府|近衛権少将]]。}}、[[近衛府|近衛権中将]]、[[中納言|権中納言]]{{Efn|摂家と同様、近衛中将から[[参議]]を経ずして[[中納言]]に[[任官|任ぜられる]]慣例となっている。}}・[[大納言|権大納言]]を経て、[[近衛大将|右近衛大将]]{{Efn|[[後白河天皇|後白河]][[院政]]末期から[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽]]院政期にかけ、摂関家の[[嫡男|嫡子]]が左大将、清華家の大納言筆頭者が右大将に[[任官]]することが例となったとされる。}}を兼ね大臣に至る。
ただし、[[江戸時代]]の太政大臣は[[摂政]]・[[関白]]経験者（[[摂家]]）に限られ、清華家から出ることはなかった{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=17}}。清華家の[[極位極官|極官]]は事実上[[左大臣]]であったが、江戸時代期を通じてわずか10例と少なく、老齢の当主が名誉的に任じられたケースばかりで在任期間も短い{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=18-19}}。[[大炊御門経光]]はわずか1日の在任であり、最長の[[花山院定好]]でも1年8ヶ月である。さらに寛延2年（1749年）の[[醍醐冬熙]]を最後に幕末まで清華家の左大臣は出ることはなかった{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=18-19}}。他の時期でも三公を摂家が占める例が多く、さらに摂家の威信拡大により清華家の大臣は評議にすら参加しないことが先例とされていた時期もあった{{sfn|高埜利彦|1989|p=21-22}}。江戸時代には清華家の極官について、摂関家側は左大臣、清華家側は太政大臣と認識していて、認識の対立があった可能性も指摘されている<ref>[[長坂良宏]]『近世の摂家と朝幕関係』（[[吉川弘文館]]、[[2018年]]） pp. 76 - 77・80・107 - 108.</ref>。
=== 明治時代から華族令廃止まで === [[1884年]]（[[明治]]17年）の[[華族令]]によって、清華の家格の家は一律に[[侯爵]]に叙されることになったが、三条家のみ[[三条実美]]の功績により[[公爵]]とされた。その後、西園寺家は[[西園寺公望]]の、徳大寺家は[[徳大寺実則]]の功績により公爵となった。
== 清華家の一覧 == === 七家 === ; [[三条家]]（転法輪家） : [[藤原北家]][[閑院流]]（太政大臣[[藤原公季]]の子孫）。[[大納言|権大納言]][[藤原公実|公実]]の二男、[[太政大臣]][[三条実行]]（[[1080年]] - [[1162年]]）が初代。清華家に次ぐ[[大臣家]]に三条家の一族を配することで権力を保った。 : [[庶家|庶流]]は大臣家の[[正親町三条家|嵯峨家]]や[[三条西家|三条西]]を始め、[[羽林家]]の[[滋野井家]]、[[姉小路家]] 等、23家にのぼる。 : [[家業]]：笛・装束。[[江戸時代]]の[[家禄]]：469石、[[家紋]]：[[唐花紋|片喰に唐花]]。近代の爵位：[[公爵]]。
; [[西園寺家]] : 藤原北家閑院流。同じく公実の三男、[[中納言|権中納言]][[藤原通季|西園寺通季]]（[[1090年]] - [[1128年]]）を祖とする。通季は母藤原光子が正妻だったため嫡子とされたが、早世したために兄弟の中でも官位が最も低かった。4代目の太政大臣[[西園寺公経|公経]]に至って、親幕派として[[承久の乱]]後権勢を誇り、[[摂家|摂関家]]から[[外戚]]の地位と[[関東申次]]の世襲職を奪った。公経は京洛北山に氏寺西園寺を建立して、家名の由来となった。 : 庶流に菊亭家、羽林家の[[清水谷家]]や[[四辻家]]や[[橋本家]]、[[大宮家 (閑院流)|大宮家]]等あり。 : 家業：[[琵琶]]。江戸時代の家禄：597石、家紋：[[巴|尾長左三つ巴]]。近代の爵位：侯爵 → 公爵。
; [[徳大寺家]] : 藤原北家閑院流。公実の四男、[[左大臣]][[徳大寺実能]]（[[1096年]] - [[1157年]]）を始祖とする。実能の嫡孫、左大臣[[徳大寺実定|実定]]は[[藤原俊成]]の妹が生んだ子で[[藤原定家|定家]]の従兄にあたり、自らも優れた歌人である。この家は[[鳥羽天皇|鳥羽]]・[[後白河天皇|後白河]][[院政]]期の[[後宮]]をほぼ独占したが、[[鎌倉時代|鎌倉]]以後やや衰えた。西園寺家と同族意識が強く、たとえば明治時代の[[西園寺公望]]は徳大寺家に生まれ西園寺家に入嗣している。 : 家業：笛、江戸時代の家禄：約410石、家紋：木瓜唐花角浮線綾。近代の爵位：侯爵 → 公爵。
; [[久我家]] : [[村上天皇]]の第8[[皇子]][[具平親王]]の男、[[右大臣]][[源師房]]（[[1008年]] - [[1077年]]）を祖とする[[村上源氏]]の嫡流。師房の姉・[[隆姫女王]]が[[関白]][[藤原頼通]]の正妻であることから頼通の異姓養子となり、頼通の異母妹[[藤原尊子 (藤原道長女)|尊子]]と結婚する。さらに師房と尊子の子・[[源顕房]]は娘の[[藤原賢子|賢子]]を[[藤原師実]]の養女とした上で[[白河天皇]]に入内させ（のち[[中宮]]）、もう一人の娘・[[藤原師子|師子]]は[[藤原忠実]]の妻となるなど、その子孫も摂関家と深い姻戚関係を築いた。 : 師房の5世孫である[[内大臣]][[源通親|通親]]は[[高階栄子|丹後局]]と組んで時の関白[[九条兼実]]を追い落とし、「{{読み仮名|'''源博陸'''|みなもと の はくりく}}」とあだ名されるほどの権勢家であった。久我家嫡流は長く[[源氏長者]]や[[淳和奨学両院別当]]を兼任したが、[[室町時代]]に入ると[[足利義満]]が源氏長者となり、[[足利将軍家]]が源氏長者となる慣例が成立する。ただし[[足利将軍家]]そのものが後継者争いなどによって不安定な状況が続いたため、実際には[[村上源氏]]公家の久我家と[[清和源氏]]武家の足利家が交互に源氏長者に就任する様相を呈し、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に入ると再び久我家が源氏長者を独占して[[久我通堅]]まで続いた。 : 「久我」の名称は、京都西南、[[山城国]][[乙訓郡]]久我（現在の[[京都市]][[伏見区]]久我）の地に別業「久我水閣」があったことが由来。 : 分家の公家諸家として、大臣家の[[中院家]]、および羽林家の[[六条家]]・[[岩倉家]]・[[千種家]]・[[東久世家]]・[[久世家]]・[[梅溪家]]・[[愛宕家]]・[[植松家]]の9家がある。 : 家業：笛、江戸時代の家禄：700石、家紋：五つ竜胆車。近代の爵位：侯爵。
; [[花山院家]] : 藤原北家師実流（花山院流）。摂政太政大臣[[藤原師実|師実]]の次子、右大臣[[花山院家忠]]（[[1062年]] - [[1136年]]）より始まる。家忠が[[花山天皇|花山上皇]]の御在所だった東一条院（[[花山院]]）を伝領したため、この家名がある。3代[[花山院忠雅|忠雅]]は朝政に明るかった上に、[[平清盛]]と親戚関係にあったことから、太政大臣という異例の昇進を遂げた。諸流に羽林家の[[中山家]]や[[野宮家]]、[[今城家]]がある。 : 家業：[[笙]]と[[筆道]]、江戸時代の家禄：約750石、家紋：[[アヤメ|菖蒲菱]]<ref name="ayame">[https://www.mitsuminejinja.or.jp/saijin/ 御祭神・由緒 | 三峯神社]</ref>。近代の爵位：侯爵。
; [[大炊御門家]] : 藤原北家師実流（花山院流）。花山院家と同じく摂政師実の子[[大炊御門経実]]（[[1068年]] - [[1131年]]）が初代。大炊御門北、万里小路東に邸宅があった。経実は権大納言どまりであったが、その子[[大炊御門経宗|経宗]]は[[二条天皇]]の{{読み仮名|[[外舅]]|がいきゅう}}として勢威をふるい、左大臣に昇って清華家の[[家格]]を確保した（経宗は閑院流公実の女公子を母とする）。 : 家業：筆道・[[和歌]]・[[和琴]]・笛・装束、江戸時代の家禄：約400石、家紋：菱に片喰草。近代の爵位：侯爵。
; [[菊亭家]]（今出川家） : 藤原北家閑院流西園寺庶流。[[鎌倉時代|鎌倉期]]の太政大臣[[西園寺実兼]]の子、左大臣[[今出川兼季|兼季]]が分家し、今出川殿を居所としたため、今出川および号として菊亭を名乗る。[[明治維新]]後は苗字の統一を図り、菊亭を名字とした。 : 家業：琵琶。:江戸時代の家禄：約1655石、家紋：三つ楓。近代の爵位：侯爵。
=== 新家 === [[江戸時代]]に七清華から2つの[[新家]]を加えたものである。しかしこの両家より[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の最高職である太政大臣に昇った例が一例もなく、既存の七清華よりも格下とされた。近代の爵位は共に侯爵であった。
; [[広幡家]] : [[源氏#正親町源氏|正親町源氏]][[嫡流]]。[[正親町天皇]]の皇孫[[八条宮智仁親王]]の第3[[王 (皇族)|王子]]、[[広幡忠幸|忠幸王]]（[[1623年]] - [[1669年]]）が[[臣籍降下]]して創立。忠幸は最初、[[尾張藩]]に赴き[[武家]]となったが、のち帰洛して大納言に進んだ。 : 禄高：約500石、家紋：[[菊花紋章|三つ葉菊]]
; [[醍醐家]] : 藤原北家摂関流。江戸時代に五摂家の[[一条家]]から分かれた家。摂政[[一条昭良]]の次男、権大納言[[醍醐冬基]]（[[1648年]] - [[1697年]]）が初代。 : 禄高：約312石、家紋：[[フジ (植物)#人間との関わり|下り藤]]
=== 絶家となった家 === ; [[洞院家]] : 藤原北家閑院流西園寺庶流。第 9代の[[洞院実熙]]の時には既に経済的に困窮。第10代[[洞院公数|公数]]は自ら絶家させる選択する。
; [[土御門家#土御門家（村上源氏）|土御門家]] : 村上源氏久我庶流。[[室町時代]]中期に[[土御門有通|有通]]が早世し絶家した。
; [[堀川家]] : 村上源氏。室町時代に絶家した。
; [[松殿家]] : 元は[[摂家]]として創設された家のひとつ。藤原氏北家嫡流の[[藤原忠通]]次男・[[松殿基房]]が祖で、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]に絶家、その後[[明和]]2年（[[1765年]]）に[[九条尚実]]の次男忠孝が九条家の分家として、清華家待遇となる松殿家を創設したが、明和5年（[[1768年]]）に嗣子無く没し、再び絶家した。

[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[Category:Qq]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|qq=}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇|qq=}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家|qq=}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚|qq=}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|qq=}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|qq=}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家|qq=}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏|qq=}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|qq=}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣|qq=}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議|qq=}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|qq=}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|qq=}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|qq=}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|qq=}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|qq=}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵|qq=}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵|qq=}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美|qq=}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則|qq=}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|qq=}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|qq=}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|qq=}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|qq=}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家|qq=}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
