== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|qq=}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|qq=}} {{Tōjō Families list|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Pages needing history import]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|qq=}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇|qq=}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家|qq=}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚|qq=}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|qq=}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|qq=}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家|qq=}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏|qq=}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|qq=}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣|qq=}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議|qq=}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|qq=}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|qq=}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|qq=}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|qq=}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|qq=}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵|qq=}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵|qq=}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美|qq=}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則|qq=}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|qq=}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|qq=}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|qq=}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|qq=}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家|qq=}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).

[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|qq=}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇|qq=}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家|qq=}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚|qq=}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|qq=}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|qq=}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家|qq=}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏|qq=}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|qq=}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣|qq=}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議|qq=}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|qq=}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|qq=}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|qq=}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|qq=}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|qq=}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵|qq=}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵|qq=}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美|qq=}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則|qq=}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|qq=}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|qq=}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|qq=}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|qq=}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家|qq=}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|qq=}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|qq=}} {{Tōjō Families list|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|qq=}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|qq=}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家|qq=}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏|qq=}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").

== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). ; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|qq=}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|1=Tōshōke families|qq=}} {{Tōjō Families list|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|qq=}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇|qq=}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家|qq=}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚|qq=}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|qq=}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|qq=}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家|qq=}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏|qq=}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|qq=}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣|qq=}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議|1=Sangi (Japan)|lt=Sangi|2=ja|3=参議|4=ru|5=Санги|6=fr|7=Sangi_(Japon)|8=ko|9=참의_(일본사)|10=es|11=Sangi|qq=}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|qq=}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|qq=}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|qq=}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|qq=}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|qq=}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵|qq=}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵|qq=}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美|qq=}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則|qq=}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|qq=}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|qq=}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|qq=}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|qq=}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家|qq=}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|qq=}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|1=Tōshōke families|qq=}} {{Tōjō Families list|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|左大臣|" to "|左大臣|1=Minister of the Left|"
revision.text:
[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|qq=}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇|qq=}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家|qq=}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚|qq=}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|qq=}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|qq=}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|qq=}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|ja|徳川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|ja|小早川氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|ja|前田氏|lt=Kaga Maeda|lt_ja=加賀前田家|qq=}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|ja|宇喜多氏|qq=}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|ja|上杉氏|qq=}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|qq=}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Inner Minister|ja|内大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Right Minister|ja|右大臣|qq=}}·{{ill|Left Minister|ja|左大臣|1=Minister of the Left|qq=}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Councillor|ja|参議|1=Sangi (Japan)|lt=Sangi|2=ja|3=参議|4=ru|5=Санги|6=fr|7=Sangi_(Japon)|8=ko|9=참의_(일본사)|10=es|11=Sangi|qq=}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|ja|中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainagon|ja|大納言|qq=}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|qq=}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|qq=}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|qq=}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|qq=}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|qq=}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|qq=}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquis|ja|侯爵|qq=}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|ja|公爵|qq=}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|ja|三条実美|qq=}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|ja|西園寺公望|qq=}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実則|qq=}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|qq=}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|ja|藤原公季|qq=}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|qq=}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|qq=}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|qq=}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|qq=}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|qq=}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anekōji family|ja|姉小路家|qq=}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|qq=}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kinsue|ja|西園寺公経|qq=}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|qq=}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|qq=}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Three-tailed tomoe|ja|巴|lt=尾長左三つ巴|lt_ja=尾長左三つ巴|qq=}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|qq=}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sadaji|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|qq=}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|ja|村上天皇|qq=}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|ja|右大臣|qq=}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|qq=}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|qq=}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|qq=}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|qq=}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|qq=}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|qq=}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|qq=}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|qq=}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosane|ja|藤原師実|qq=}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|qq=}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|qq=}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|qq=}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|qq=}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|shō|ja|笙|qq=}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|qq=}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|qq=}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|qq=}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|qq=}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akisuke|ja|一条昭良|qq=}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|ja|醍醐冬基|qq=}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|qq=}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代 (日本)|qq=}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|kazoku|ja|華族|qq=}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|qq=}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke|ja|藤原北家|qq=}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|qq=}} * {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|1=Tōshōke families|qq=}} {{Tōjō Families list|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Seiga families]] '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|qq=}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|ja|西園寺家|lt=Saionji|lt_ja=西園寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|lt=Tokudaiji|lt_ja=徳大寺|qq=}}・{{ill|Kuga family|ja|久我家|lt=Kuga|lt_ja=久我|qq=}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|qq=}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|qq=}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|qq=}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|qq=}} and become {{ill|Chancellor of the Realm|ja|太政大臣|qq=}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|qq=}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|qq=}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|ja|後三条天皇|qq=}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Regent Houses|ja|摂家|1=Five regent houses|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses|lt_ja=摂関家|qq=}} lost their status as {{ill|maternal relatives|ja|外戚|qq=}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake.
