<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367650]] Wikidata name: Seigake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|1=Daijinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438579}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}}・{{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|1=kinda'chi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392224}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|WD=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses}} lost their status as {{ill|Consort kin|en|Consort kin|WD=Q1050320|lt=maternal relatives}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 院政|1=cloistered rule|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q865416}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hokke}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|1=Toyotomi government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634225}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|en|Tokugawa clan|WD=Q1412769|lt=Tokugawa clan}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|en|Kobayakawa clan|WD=Q2975298|lt=Kobayakawa clan}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|en|Maeda clan|WD=Q578033|lt=Kaga Maeda}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|en|Ukita clan|WD=Q2975390|lt=Ukita clan}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Inner Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Right Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}, {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中納言|1=Chūnagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185382}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大納言|1=Dainagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1354706}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|1=Peerage Law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620084}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquess|en|Marquess|WD=Q209726|lt=Marquis}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|en|Duke|WD=Q166886|lt=Duke}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|en|Sanjō Sanetomi|WD=Q516646|lt=Sanjō Sanetomi}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|en|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|WD=Q5358097|lt=Tokudaiji Sanetsune}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|en|Fujiwara no Kinsue|WD=Q3090629|lt=Fujiwara no Kinsue}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|1=Fujiwara no Kinzane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10514083}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|1=Sanjō Saneyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355837}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|1=Ōgimachisanjō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545676}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|1=Sanjōnishi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355857}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|1=Shigenoi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564989}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anenokōji clan|en|Anenokōji clan|WD=Q2975216|lt=Anekōji family}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|1=karahanamon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21550705}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|1=Fujiwara no Michisue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623587}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kintsune|en|Saionji Kintsune|WD=Q3464635|lt=Saionji Kinsue}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|1=Shimizudani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561185}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 室町家|1=Muromachi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453057}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|1=Ōmiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434416}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tomoe|en|Tomoe|WD=Q1557435|lt=尾長左三つ巴}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|1=Tokudaiji Saneyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489586}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanesada|en|Tokudaiji Sanesada|WD=Q3530487|lt=Tokudaiji Sadaji}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|1=Nakanoin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367812}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|1=Rokujō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392411}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|1=Iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474158}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|1=Chigusa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405808}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|1=Higashikuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523897}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|1=Kuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368902}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|1=Umetani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122863}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|1=Otagi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122862}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|1=Uematsu family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540623}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morozane|en|Fujiwara no Morozane|WD=Q1366049|lt=Fujiwara no Morosane}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原家忠|1=Fujiwara no Ietada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623189}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原忠雅|1=Fujiwara no Tadamasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623265}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|1=Nakayama family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11364137}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|1=Nonomiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645309}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|1=Imaki family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122861}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|Shō (instrument)|en|Shō (instrument)|WD=Q1975355|lt=shō}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経実|1=Fujiwara no Tsunezane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623460}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経宗|1=Fujiwara no Tsunemune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623461}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|1=Saionji Sanekane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223333}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|1=Imadegawa Kanesue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223017}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akiyoshi|en|Ichijō Akiyoshi|WD=Q2410760|lt=Ichijō Akisuke}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|en|Daigo Fuyumoto|WD=Q5209111|lt=Daigo Fuyumoto}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|1=Matsudono family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531532}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|WD=Q241734|lt=kazoku}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 摂家|1=Five regent houses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067142}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hokke}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} * {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|1=Tōshōke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11427847}} {{Tōshōke families|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q5367650}} [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:Murakami Genji clan]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367650]] Wikidata name: Seigake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|1=Daijinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438579}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}}・{{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|1=kinda'chi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392224}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|WD=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses}} lost their status as {{ill|Consort kin|en|Consort kin|WD=Q1050320|lt=maternal relatives}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 院政|1=cloistered rule|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q865416}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hokke}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|1=Toyotomi government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634225}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|en|Tokugawa clan|WD=Q1412769|lt=Tokugawa clan}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|en|Kobayakawa clan|WD=Q2975298|lt=Kobayakawa clan}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|en|Maeda clan|WD=Q578033|lt=Kaga Maeda}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|en|Ukita clan|WD=Q2975390|lt=Ukita clan}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Inner Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Right Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}, {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中納言|1=Chūnagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185382}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大納言|1=Dainagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1354706}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|1=Peerage Law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620084}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquess|en|Marquess|WD=Q209726|lt=Marquis}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|en|Duke|WD=Q166886|lt=Duke}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|en|Sanjō Sanetomi|WD=Q516646|lt=Sanjō Sanetomi}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|en|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|WD=Q5358097|lt=Tokudaiji Sanetsune}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|en|Fujiwara no Kinsue|WD=Q3090629|lt=Fujiwara no Kinsue}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|1=Fujiwara no Kinzane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10514083}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|1=Sanjō Saneyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355837}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|1=Ōgimachisanjō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545676}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|1=Sanjōnishi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355857}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|1=Shigenoi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564989}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anenokōji clan|en|Anenokōji clan|WD=Q2975216|lt=Anekōji family}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|1=karahanamon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21550705}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|1=Fujiwara no Michisue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623587}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kintsune|en|Saionji Kintsune|WD=Q3464635|lt=Saionji Kinsue}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|1=Shimizudani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561185}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 室町家|1=Muromachi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453057}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|1=Ōmiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434416}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tomoe|en|Tomoe|WD=Q1557435|lt=尾長左三つ巴}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|1=Tokudaiji Saneyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489586}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanesada|en|Tokudaiji Sanesada|WD=Q3530487|lt=Tokudaiji Sadaji}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|1=Nakanoin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367812}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|1=Rokujō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392411}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|1=Iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474158}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|1=Chigusa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405808}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|1=Higashikuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523897}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|1=Kuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368902}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|1=Umetani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122863}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|1=Otagi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122862}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|1=Uematsu family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540623}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morozane|en|Fujiwara no Morozane|WD=Q1366049|lt=Fujiwara no Morosane}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原家忠|1=Fujiwara no Ietada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623189}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原忠雅|1=Fujiwara no Tadamasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623265}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|1=Nakayama family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11364137}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|1=Nonomiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645309}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|1=Imaki family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122861}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|Shō (instrument)|en|Shō (instrument)|WD=Q1975355|lt=shō}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経実|1=Fujiwara no Tsunezane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623460}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経宗|1=Fujiwara no Tsunemune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623461}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|1=Saionji Sanekane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223333}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|1=Imadegawa Kanesue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223017}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akiyoshi|en|Ichijō Akiyoshi|WD=Q2410760|lt=Ichijō Akisuke}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|en|Daigo Fuyumoto|WD=Q5209111|lt=Daigo Fuyumoto}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|1=Matsudono family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531532}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q5367650]] Wikidata name: Seigake [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|1=Daijinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438579}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}}・{{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|1=kinda'chi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392224}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|WD=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses}} lost their status as {{ill|Consort kin|en|Consort kin|WD=Q1050320|lt=maternal relatives}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 院政|1=cloistered rule|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q865416}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hokke}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|1=Toyotomi government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634225}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|en|Tokugawa clan|WD=Q1412769|lt=Tokugawa clan}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|en|Kobayakawa clan|WD=Q2975298|lt=Kobayakawa clan}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|en|Maeda clan|WD=Q578033|lt=Kaga Maeda}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|en|Ukita clan|WD=Q2975390|lt=Ukita clan}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Inner Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Right Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}, {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中納言|1=Chūnagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185382}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大納言|1=Dainagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1354706}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|1=Peerage Law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620084}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquess|en|Marquess|WD=Q209726|lt=Marquis}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|en|Duke|WD=Q166886|lt=Duke}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|en|Sanjō Sanetomi|WD=Q516646|lt=Sanjō Sanetomi}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|en|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|WD=Q5358097|lt=Tokudaiji Sanetsune}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|en|Fujiwara no Kinsue|WD=Q3090629|lt=Fujiwara no Kinsue}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|1=Fujiwara no Kinzane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10514083}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|1=Sanjō Saneyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355837}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|1=Ōgimachisanjō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545676}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|1=Sanjōnishi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355857}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|1=Shigenoi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564989}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anenokōji clan|en|Anenokōji clan|WD=Q2975216|lt=Anekōji family}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|1=karahanamon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21550705}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|1=Fujiwara no Michisue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623587}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kintsune|en|Saionji Kintsune|WD=Q3464635|lt=Saionji Kinsue}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|1=Shimizudani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561185}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 室町家|1=Muromachi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453057}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|1=Ōmiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434416}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tomoe|en|Tomoe|WD=Q1557435|lt=尾長左三つ巴}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|1=Tokudaiji Saneyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489586}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanesada|en|Tokudaiji Sanesada|WD=Q3530487|lt=Tokudaiji Sadaji}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|1=Nakanoin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367812}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|1=Rokujō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392411}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|1=Iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474158}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|1=Chigusa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405808}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|1=Higashikuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523897}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|1=Kuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368902}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|1=Umetani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122863}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|1=Otagi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122862}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|1=Uematsu family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540623}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morozane|en|Fujiwara no Morozane|WD=Q1366049|lt=Fujiwara no Morosane}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原家忠|1=Fujiwara no Ietada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623189}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原忠雅|1=Fujiwara no Tadamasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623265}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|1=Nakayama family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11364137}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|1=Nonomiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645309}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|1=Imaki family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122861}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|Shō (instrument)|en|Shō (instrument)|WD=Q1975355|lt=shō}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経実|1=Fujiwara no Tsunezane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623460}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経宗|1=Fujiwara no Tsunemune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623461}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|1=Saionji Sanekane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223333}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|1=Imadegawa Kanesue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223017}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
; {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} : Direct descendants of the main line of the Otokunonokuni Genji. Founded by Prince Hui Prince Hirohata, eighth son of Prince Toshihito of the Ōinonomiya lineage, who took the surname Hirohata (1623–1669) and later served as a Dainagon. : Stipend: approx. 500 koku. Family crest: three-leaf chrysanthemum.
; {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} : A branch of the Ichijō family (one of the Five Regent Houses). Founded by the second son of Regent {{ill|Ichijō Akiyoshi|en|Ichijō Akiyoshi|WD=Q2410760|lt=Ichijō Akisuke}}, Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Daigo Fuyumoto|en|Daigo Fuyumoto|WD=Q5209111|lt=Daigo Fuyumoto}} (1648–1697). : Stipend: approx. 312 koku. Family crest: downward wisteria.
=== Houses that Became Extinct === ; {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. By the 9th head, Dōin Ienobu, the family was already in financial distress; the 10th head, Dōin Kinnori, chose to end the lineage.
; {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} : A cadet branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the mid-{{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} when Tsuchimikado Ariyuki died young.
; {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} : A branch of the Murakami Genji. The lineage ended in the Muromachi period.
; {{ill|Matsudono family|ja|松殿家|1=Matsudono family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531532}} : Originally founded as one of the Regent Houses by Fujiwara no Tadamichi’s second son, Matsudono Motofusa. The lineage became extinct during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, but was briefly reestablished in Meiwa 2 (1765) as a branch of the Kujo family, only to become extinct again in Meiwa 5 (1768).
== Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ|qq=}} === Annotations === {{Notelist|qq=}} === Sources === {{Reflist|qq=}}
== References == * {{Cite journal|和書|title = 「禁中並公家諸法度」についての一考察 : 公家の家格をめぐって|url = https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050564287965246720|publisher = 学習院大学史料館|journal = 学習院大学史料館紀要|naid = 110007875662|issn = 02890860|author = 高埜利彦|authorlink = 高埜利彦|year = 1989|volume = 5|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|WD=Q241734|lt=kazoku}} * {{ill|Five Regent Houses|ja|五摂家|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 摂家|1=Five regent houses|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067142}} * {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hokke}} * {{ill|Murakami Genji|ja|村上源氏|1=Murakami-Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3867344}} * {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} * {{ill|Dōjō-ke|ja|堂上家|1=Tōshōke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11427847}} {{Tōshōke families|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|清華家|version=103419371|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q5367650}} [[Category:Court Noble Families]] [[Category:Kuge]] [[Category:Murakami Genji clan]] [[Category:Seiga families]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

revision.comment: imported only latest revision from https://archive.org/details/miraheze-wiki-shintowiki-20251218 to avoid excessive disk usage, comment on the revision was Removing {{afc comment}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:清華家]][[ko:청화가]][[zh:清华家]]
'''Seigake''' is one of the ranks of the court nobles . It is ranked above the {{ill|Ministerial Houses|ja|大臣家|1=Daijinke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438579}} , after the top five regent families . It refers to seven main families ( {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}}・{{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}}・{{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}}・{{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}}・{{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}}・{{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}}・{{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} ) who could serve as both {{ill|Minister and General|ja|大臣・大将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and become {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} (there were ten families in the Muromachi period, and nine in the Edo period, including the {{ill|Hirohata family|ja|広幡家|lt=Hirohata|lt_ja=広幡|1=Hirohata family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485396}} and {{ill|Daigo family|ja|醍醐家|lt=Daigo|lt_ja=醍醐|1=Daigo Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17208869}} families). The number of families with this rank varied depending on the era). [[ja:清華家]] [[ko:청화가]] [[zh:清华家]] == Process of Establishment == '''Also called Eiyō-ke or Kazoku.''' The sons and daughters of the Regent Houses and the Seigake families were called {{ill|kinda'chi|ja|公達|1=kinda'chi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392224}}. From the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Sanjō|en|Emperor Go-Sanjō|WD=Q45844|lt=Emperor Go-Sanjō}} onward<ref>…</ref>, the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Regent and Kampaku Houses}} lost their status as {{ill|Consort kin|en|Consort kin|WD=Q1050320|lt=maternal relatives}}, and in their place, those lineages that became maternal relatives formed the original mold of the family rank later called Seigake. A family rank equivalent to Seigake had already been established by the {{ill|cloistered rule period|ja|院政期|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 院政|1=cloistered rule|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q865416}}. Therefore, in addition to being able to be promoted to Minister and General, the Seigake rank also carries the qualification that one's daughters are eligible to become Empress consorts.
The so-called "Seven Seigake" refers to the fact that, through the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, many lineages holding the Seigake rank (for example, the {{ill|Yamashina family|ja|山階家|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Dōin family|ja|洞院家|1=Tōin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555876}} of the Kan'in branch of the {{ill|Fujiwara Hok-ke|en|Fujiwara Hok-ke|WD=Q2975268|lt=Fujiwara Hokke}}; and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|lt=Tsuchimikado|lt_ja=土御門家（村上源氏）|qq=}} and {{ill|Horikawa family|ja|堀川家|qq=}} of the Murakami Genji’s Akinobu lineage) either died out or lost the Seigake rank, resulting in only seven houses remaining in the end. This means that the roster of families was not fixed from the beginning.
Under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|1=Toyotomi government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11634225}}, the military lords {{ill|Tokugawa clan|en|Tokugawa clan|WD=Q1412769|lt=Tokugawa clan}}·{{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|WD=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan}}·{{ill|Kobayakawa clan|en|Kobayakawa clan|WD=Q2975298|lt=Kobayakawa clan}}·{{ill|Maeda clan|en|Maeda clan|WD=Q578033|lt=Kaga Maeda}}·{{ill|Ukita clan|en|Ukita clan|WD=Q2975390|lt=Ukita clan}}·{{ill|Uesugi clan|en|Uesugi clan|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi clan}} were regarded as having achieved Seigake rank and were treated as equivalent ("warrior Seigake families").
== Promotion Process == === From the Kamakura Period to the Azuchi–Momoyama Period === They concurrently held the positions of Left and Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}} and Minister, proceeded through {{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Inner Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Right Minister}}·{{ill|Minister of the Left|en|Minister of the Left|WD=Q949008|lt=Minister of the Left}}, and could be promoted as high as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}}. When the Seigake rank first emerged in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the families that later comprised the Seven Seigake and other lineages holding the Seigake rank were initially appointed as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Sangi}}, then promoted to {{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}, {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, Inner Minister, Right Minister, Left Minister, and Chancellor of the Realm. Later, similar to the Regent Houses, by the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}), a custom was established of appointing to Chūnagon without first holding the Councillor rank.<ref>…</ref>
=== Edo Period === In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, starting from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade and Chamberlain ({{ill|Chamberlain|ja|侍従|1=Chamberlain of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2948088}}), they proceeded to {{ill|Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権少将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, then {{ill|Assistant Guard Captain|ja|近衛府権中将|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Provisional Chūnagon|ja|権中納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中納言|1=Chūnagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185382}}, {{ill|Provisional Dainagon|ja|権大納言|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大納言|1=Dainagon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1354706}}, concurrently holding Right {{ill|Imperial Guard Commander|ja|右近衛大将|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛大将|1=Konoe Daisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638972}}, reaching the Ministerial rank. However, in the Edo period, the {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Chancellor of the Realm}} was limited to those who had served as Regent or Kampaku (Regent Houses), and no Seigake family ever attained this post. The pinnacle official rank for the Seigake families was effectively the Left Minister, but throughout the Edo period there were only ten examples—honorary appointments of elderly heads of families with very short tenures.<ref>…</ref>
=== From the Meiji Period to the Abolition of the Peerage System === Under the {{ill|Peerage Act of 1884|ja|華族令|1=Peerage Law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11620084}} (Meiji 17), all families holding the Seigake rank were uniformly conferred the title of {{ill|Marquess|en|Marquess|WD=Q209726|lt=Marquis}}; however, the Sanjō family alone was elevated to {{ill|Duke|en|Duke|WD=Q166886|lt=Duke}} due to the achievements of {{ill|Sanjō Sanetomi|en|Sanjō Sanetomi|WD=Q516646|lt=Sanjō Sanetomi}}. Subsequently, the Saionji family and the Tokudaiji family were also raised to dukedom in recognition of the contributions of {{ill|Saionji Kinmochi|en|Saionji Kinmochi|WD=Q297628|lt=Saionji Kinmochi}} and {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|en|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|WD=Q5358097|lt=Tokudaiji Sanetsune}}, respectively.
== List of Seigake Families == === The Seven Houses === ; {{ill|Sanjō family|ja|三条家|lt=Sanjō|lt_ja=三条|qq=}} (Tenporin family) : {{ill|Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch|ja|藤原北家閑院流|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Kinsue|en|Fujiwara no Kinsue|WD=Q3090629|lt=Fujiwara no Kinsue}}). The second son of Provisional Dainagon {{ill|Fujiwara no Kimitsu|ja|藤原公実|1=Fujiwara no Kinzane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10514083}}, {{ill|Sanjō Saneyuki|ja|三条実行|1=Sanjō Saneyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355837}} (1080–1162) was the first head. They maintained power by placing members of the Sanjō family in the ministerial houses next in rank to the Seigake. : {{ill|Cadet houses|ja|庶家|lt=Cadet lineages|lt_ja=庶流|1=shoke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486238}} include the {{ill|Ōinonomachi-Sanjō family|ja|正親町三条家|lt=Ōinonomachi-Sanjō|lt_ja=嵯峨家|1=Ōgimachisanjō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545676}} and {{ill|Sanjo Nishi family|ja|三条西家|lt=Sanjo Nishi|lt_ja=三条西|1=Sanjōnishi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355857}} of the Ministerial Houses, the {{ill|Shigenoi branch|ja|滋野井家|1=Shigenoi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564989}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Anenokōji clan|en|Anenokōji clan|WD=Q2975216|lt=Anekōji family}}, etc., totaling 23 families. : Family traditions: flute and court attire. Edo period stipend: 469 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tang flower and katakushi motif|ja|唐花紋|lt=片喰に唐花|lt_ja=片喰に唐花|1=karahanamon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21550705}}. Modern peerage: Duke.
; {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} : A branch of the Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the third son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Provisional Chūnagon {{ill|Saionji Michitsune|ja|藤原通季|1=Fujiwara no Michisue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623587}} (1090–1128). Michitsune’s mother was Fujiwara no Mitsuko, the principal wife, making him the heir despite being the youngest. The fourth-generation Chancellor {{ill|Saionji Kintsune|en|Saionji Kintsune|WD=Q3464635|lt=Saionji Kinsue}} asserted his power as a pro-shogunate figure after the Jōkyū War, wresting the positions of maternal relative and the hereditary post of Kantō shinjū from the Regent Houses. He founded the family's ancestral temple, Saionji, on the northern mountains of Kyoto, from which the family takes its name. : Cadet branches include the Kikutei family, the {{ill|Shimizutani family|ja|清水谷家|1=Shimizudani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561185}} and {{ill|Yotsutsuji family|ja|四辻家|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 室町家|1=Muromachi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453057}} of the Hyōrin family, the {{ill|Ōmiya family|ja|大宮家|1=Ōmiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11434416}}, etc. : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: 597 koku. Family crest: {{ill|Tomoe|en|Tomoe|WD=Q1557435|lt=尾長左三つ巴}}. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Tokudaiji family|en|Tokudaiji family|WD=Q11489589|lt=Tokudaiji}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Kan'in branch. Founded by the fourth son of Fujiwara no Kinsue, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanenō|ja|徳大寺実能|1=Tokudaiji Saneyoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489586}} (1096–1157). His grandson, Left Minister {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanesada|en|Tokudaiji Sanesada|WD=Q3530487|lt=Tokudaiji Sadaji}}, was a noted poet and cousin of Fujiwara no Teika. This family monopolized the rear palace (kōkyū) during the cloistered reigns of Emperors Toba and Go-Shirakawa but declined somewhat after the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}. The Saionji and Tokudaiji families remained closely connected; for example, Saionji Kinmochi was born into the Tokudaiji family and adopted into the Saionji family. : Family traditions: flute. Edo period stipend: approx. 410 koku. Family crest: mokkō tang flower pattern. Modern peerage: Marquis→Duke.
; {{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|WD=Q464377|lt=Kuga}} : Direct descendants of {{ill|Prince Guhira|ja|具平親王|1=Tomohira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11393729}}, the eighth son of {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}}, and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} {{ill|Minamoto no Morofusa|ja|源師房|1=Minamoto no Morofusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11564069}} (1008–1077). Morofusa’s sister, Princess Takahime, was the principal wife of Regent Fujiwara no Yorimichi, leading Morofusa to become adoptive child of Yorimichi and marry Yorimichi’s half-sister, Fujiwara no Toshiko. His grandson Minamoto no Akifusa’s daughter, Fujiwara no Kenshi, became consort to Emperor Shirakawa, and another daughter married Fujiwara no Tadazane, forging deep ties with the Regent Houses. : In the Muromachi period, appointments to the hereditary office of Governor-General alternating between the Kuga family of the Murakami Genji and the Ashikaga shogunate became customary, though during the Sengoku period the Kuga family regained exclusive occupancy until Kuga Michikata. : The name "Kuga" derives from their villa "Kuga Suikyaku" in Kuga, Otokuni District, Yamashiro Province (present-day Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City). : Cadet branches include the {{ill|Nakoin family|ja|中院家|1=Nakanoin family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367812}} (a Ministerial House) and nine Hyōrin family branches: {{ill|Rokujō family|ja|六条家|1=Rokujō family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392411}}, {{ill|Iwakura family|ja|岩倉家|1=Iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11474158}}, {{ill|Chigusa family|ja|千種家|1=Chigusa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405808}}, {{ill|Higashikuse family|ja|東久世家|1=Higashikuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11523897}}, {{ill|Kuze family|ja|久世家|1=Kuze family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11368902}}, {{ill|Baikei family|ja|梅溪家|1=Umetani family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122863}}, {{ill|Atago family|ja|愛宕家|1=Otagi family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122862}}, {{ill|Uematsu family|ja|植松家|1=Uematsu family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540623}}. : Family tradition: flute. Edo period stipend: 700 koku. Family crest: five gentian wheels. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kazan'in family|ja|花山院家|lt=Kazan'in|lt_ja=花山院|1=Kazan'in family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615289}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by the second son of Regent–Chancellor {{ill|Fujiwara no Morozane|en|Fujiwara no Morozane|WD=Q1366049|lt=Fujiwara no Morosane}}, Right Minister {{ill|Kazan'in Ietada|ja|花山院家忠|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原家忠|1=Fujiwara no Ietada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623189}} (1062–1136). Ietada inherited the former palace of Emperor Kazan (the Higashiichijō-in, known as Kazan'in), giving the family its name. The third head, {{ill|Kazan'in Tadamasa|ja|花山院忠雅|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原忠雅|1=Fujiwara no Tadamasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623265}}, an accomplished courtier and relative of Taira no Kiyomori, achieved the rare promotion to Chancellor of the Realm. Cadet lines include the {{ill|Nakayama family|ja|中山家|1=Nakayama family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11364137}}, {{ill|Nomiya family|ja|野宮家|1=Nonomiya family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645309}}, and {{ill|Imashiro family|ja|今城家|1=Imaki family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22122861}} of the Hyōrin family. : Family traditions: {{ill|Shō (instrument)|en|Shō (instrument)|WD=Q1975355|lt=shō}} and calligraphy (brushwork). Edo period stipend: approx. 750 koku. Family crest: iris diamond<ref name="ayame">…</ref>. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Ōimikado family|ja|大炊御門家|lt=Ōimikado|lt_ja=大炊御門|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} : Fujiwara Hokke, Saneie lineage (Kazan'in branch). Founded by {{ill|Ōimikado Tsuneyori|ja|大炊御門経実|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経実|1=Fujiwara no Tsunezane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623460}} (1068–1131), son of Regent Morosane. The family mansion was located north of the Banri-koji gate; Tsuneyori’s son {{ill|Ōimikado Tsunesō|ja|大炊御門経宗|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藤原経宗|1=Fujiwara no Tsunemune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623461}} rose to Left Minister as the emperor’s son-in-law, securing the Seigake rank (his mother was the daughter of Fujiwara no Kimimune of the Kan'in branch). : Family traditions: calligraphy, waka poetry, koto, flute, court attire. Edo period stipend: approx. 400 koku. Family crest: katakushi in a lozenge. Modern peerage: Marquis.
; {{ill|Kikutei family|ja|菊亭家|lt=Kikutei|lt_ja=菊亭|1=Kikutei family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619510}} (Imadegawa family) : A cadet branch of the Saionji family. Founded when Left Minister {{ill|Saionji Sanemoto|ja|西園寺実兼|1=Saionji Sanekane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223333}}’s son {{ill|Imadegawa Kanesue|ja|今出川兼季|1=Imadegawa Kanesue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7223017}} (Kamakura period) established his residence at Imadegawa, adopting the name Kikutei. After the Meiji Restoration, the family unified its surname as "Kikutei." : Family tradition: biwa. Edo period stipend: approx. 1655 koku. Family crest: three-maple. Modern peerage: Marquis.
=== New Houses === Two additional houses were added in the Edo period but never produced a Chancellor of the Realm; they were considered lower than the existing Seven Houses. Both held the title of Marquis in modern times.
